data.title: Visa, Mastercard end in the red after six-session rally
data.description:
Payment processing firms Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) snapped six straight sessions of gains on Wednesday. The former lost 0.11% to end at $320.47, while the latter closed 0.27% lower at $522.92.

Since February 24, Visa has gained 4.43%, while Mastercard added 5.29%. The rise came after stocks of both companies declined heavily in the previous session on the back of a research report that revived fears that stablecoin-based technology will obviate the need for traditional card networks. V registered a 4.50% fall, while MA plunged 5.77%

Specific to credit card payment stocks, the Citrini Research note theorized that agentic AI turned to stablecoin-based systems to execute transactions more cheaply.

However, Seeking Alpha analyst Secret Compounders defended Mastercard, arguing that the article ignores deeply ingrained consumer habits and Mastercard's core value proposition.

"Consumers actively prefer credit cards because they view the friction as a benefit, not a problem. They love being able to easily dispute charges at the end of the month while earning cash back on every swipe. The 2-3% interchange fee is certainly a complaint for the merchants who pay it. Today, many alternative payment systems are formed because of merchant pushback,” it said.

MA’s value-added services segment, which includes cybersecurity and data monetization, is a key and often overlooked growth driver, the analyst additionally noted.

Meanwhile, Ray Merola was bullish on Visa with a Buy rating on the back of record outstanding financial and operational results.

Both companies have been rated as Buy by Seeking Alpha analysts, while Wall Street analysts have issued a Strong Buy call. As per Seeking Alpha’s quant rating, both V and MA have been rated Hold.

data.title: NRC Health says total recurring contract value exceeds $152 million
data.description:
• National Research (NRC) said on Wednesday its total recurring contract value (TRCV) exceeded $152 million as of March 4, representing a 6% increase since the end of 2025 and a 13% rise year over year.
  • The company said TRCV reflects revenue expected to be recognized over the next 12 months from renewable contracts.
  • NRC Health said the milestone does not represent its full financial results for the quarter ending March 31, 2026.
  • NRC +0.15% after hours to $13.14.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Broadcom in charts: Revenue from semiconductor solutions rises 52% Y/Y
ReGen III launches $4M private placement financing
data.description:
• ReGen III (GIII:CA) has launched a non-brokered private placement of up to 20M units at $0.20/unit.
  • The offering could generate gross proceeds of up to $4M.
  • Net proceeds will be used for working capital, general corporate purposes, and debt repayment.
  • Each unit consists of one common share and one-half of one common share purchase warrant.
  • Each whole warrant allows the holder to purchase one additional common share at $0.30 per share.
  • The warrants are exercisable for a period of three years from the date of issuance.

data.title: EyePoint outlines mid-2026 topline data target for DURAVYU in wet AMD and DME as Phase III trials advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: EyePoint, Inc. (EYPT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jay Duker, President and CEO, stated that “2025 was defined by significant progress and achievement for EyePoint as we made important advances that set the stage for success and potential value creation for the year ahead.” He highlighted that DURAVYU is on track to deliver top line data in wet AMD beginning in mid-2026 and that first patients were dosed in both pivotal Phase III DME trials. Duker also emphasized a strong cash position expected to fund operations into Q4 2027 and described the ongoing transition to a commercial organization with the appointment of Michael Campbell as Chief Commercial Officer.
  • Duker outlined that DURAVYU’s clinical profile is “compelling,” citing durability, improved vision, anatomical control, and a favorable safety record in over 190 patients. He also noted that the drug’s unique mechanism, “inhibits VEGF, PDGF and IL-6 via the JAK1 receptor with no TIE-2 inhibition,” which is believed to address both vascular leakage and inflammation in retinal diseases.
  • The company is preparing for regulatory filings, with operations expanding at its Northbridge, Massachusetts cGMP facility and readiness for pre-approval inspection.
  • George Elston, Executive VP & CFO, stated, “We ended 2025 with a strong balance sheet of $306 million in cash and investments, driven by continued stewardship of our resources and $173 million follow-on financing in October.”

Outlook

  • Duker indicated that EyePoint expects to report top line data for the Phase III LUGANO trial in mid-2026 with LUCIA data to follow closely, complete enrollment in the pivotal Phase III DME program in the second half of 2026, and prepare for regulatory filing in wet AMD, assuming positive data.
  • The company plans to fund operations into the fourth quarter of 2027, covering key milestones including NDA preparation for the wet AMD program and fully funding the DME program.

Financial Results

  • Elston reported total net revenue for Q4 2025 was $0.6 million compared to $11.6 million in Q4 2024, with the decrease primarily attributed to the recognition of remaining deferred revenue from the YUTIQ product rights license agreement.
  • Operating expenses for Q4 2025 totaled $71 million compared to $57 million in the prior year period, mainly due to ongoing Phase III trials for DURAVYU.
  • Net nonoperating income was $3 million and net loss for the quarter was approximately $68 million or $0.81 per share, compared to a net loss of $41 million or $0.64 per share in the prior year period.
  • For the full year 2025, total net revenue was $31 million versus $43 million in 2024, and operating expenses were $275 million compared to $189 million, reflecting increased development activity. Net loss for the year was $232 million or $3.17 per share, compared to $131 million or $2.32 per share in 2024. Cash and investments at year-end totaled $306 million.

Q&A

  • Tessa Romero, JPMorgan: Asked about ocular adverse events and physician safety feedback. Duker responded that among 191 patients, “the number of cataracts...is 5.8%,” and that “in the controlled DAVIO 2 trial, there was no mismatch between the cataracts at all.” For vitreous floaters, “5.2% of the DURAVYU patients reported floaters...consistent with what you might see in any type of study that has injections into the eye.” Duker added, “we're very comfortable with our safety. We've had no ocular or systemic SAEs attributed to our drug.”
  • Yatin Suneja, Guggenheim: Inquired about regulatory strategy and safety signals. Duker noted, “we have 2 identical Phase III wet AMD trials underway...If, in fact, the FDA would allow us to file with a single trial, our second trial is only 2 months behind,” but EyePoint does not anticipate altering its approach due to global regulatory requirements.
  • Yigal Nochomovitz, Citigroup: Asked about safety monitoring cadence and biomarker work. CMO Ramiro Ribeiro explained the next DMC meeting is scheduled for May and that safety profile has been consistent. Duker mentioned ongoing biomarker research with data to be presented at ARVO in May.
  • Yuxi Dong, Jefferies: Asked about DURAVYU's multi-mechanism profile in regulatory and commercial messaging. Ribeiro stated that the regulatory approach is noninferiority, but superiority on BCVA will be tested. Michael Campbell, CCO, said the MOA offers “a real meaningful commercial opportunity to really separate yourself in the marketplace.”
  • Graig Suvannavejh, Mizuho: Asked about commercial preparation. Campbell detailed a go-to-market strategy focused on patient access and payer engagement. Ribeiro clarified the differences in Phase III DME and wet AMD trial designs, notably dosing schedules.
  • Debanjana Chatterjee, JonesTrading: Asked about intraocular inflammation. Duker reported, “We had 2 cases of iritis...both cases were mild, treated with topical drops and resolved quickly...no reported cases of uveitis, no reported cases of vitritis.”
  • Colleen Hanley, Robert W. Baird: Asked about measurement of reduction in treatment burden. Duker said, “the reduction in treatment burden is to be measured after the load...we would expect a 60% reduction in treatment burden in the DURAVYU arm,” but with supplementation, “we would have an approximate 40% reduction.” Ribeiro added that superiority testing for treatment burden is included.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on safety, regulatory clarity, and differentiation versus competitors. Most questions were fact-finding and sought further detail on trial design and commercial readiness.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone, emphasizing clinical progress, safety, and strategic positioning. Duker repeatedly referenced robust safety and efficacy data and the derisked nature of the development path, stating, “We believe that DURAVYU is well positioned to be the first to market among all current investigational sustained release programs.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts retained a similar tone, but with increased focus on commercial readiness, operational expansion, and data-driven expectations for 2026 milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • This quarter, management highlighted the dosing of first patients in pivotal Phase III DME trials and the appointment of a new Chief Commercial Officer, signaling a shift toward commercialization.
  • Guidance remains focused on mid-2026 topline data for wet AMD and late 2027 for DME, with continued confidence in cash runway into Q4 2027.
  • Financial results showed lower revenue and higher operating expenses versus the previous quarter, reflecting increased trial activity.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter shifted more toward safety outcomes, operational scalability, and commercial planning, with slightly more emphasis on practical aspects of launch and physician adoption.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited no new safety signals in ongoing trials and reported low rates of adverse events such as cataracts (5.8%) and vitreous floaters (5.2%).
  • Duker addressed concerns about intraocular inflammation, reporting only two mild cases of iritis out of 191 patients.
  • Regulatory risk remains, but Duker expressed confidence in the derisked approach with parallel Phase III trials and alignment with global regulatory standards.
  • Analysts probed for potential barriers to adoption, including step therapy and initial patient selection, but management stated they anticipate initial use in high-burden patients with expansion pending further data.

Final Takeaway

EyePoint’s fourth quarter 2025 call underscores momentum in advancing DURAVYU through late-stage clinical development for wet AMD and DME, with top line data in wet AMD targeted for mid-2026. Management emphasized robust safety, differentiated multi-mechanism action, and a strong balance sheet supporting a transition to commercial operations. The addition of a new Chief Commercial Officer and progress in Phase III DME trials mark a strategic shift toward launch readiness as the company prepares for key clinical and regulatory milestones in 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nexxen outlines over 8% contribution ex-TAC growth target for 2026 as enterprise and CTV drive momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nexxen International Ltd. (NEXN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ofer Druker stated that Nexxen "met our updated full year guidance and are seeing strong momentum in early 2026," highlighting record January and February performance. Druker attributed this to "infrastructure investments made in 2025 to support long-term programmatic trading growth" and new partnerships with leading DSPs, especially in CTV and enterprise solutions. He emphasized the launch of the "industry's first programmatic Smart TV on screen advertising solution" with VI (formerly VIDAA) and The Trade Desk, describing it as "a pivotal milestone in expanding high-quality programmatic advertising on the open Internet through a new engaging channel that is more resistant to AI-driven disruption."
  • Druker also discussed broadening AI integration: "Customers are achieving better results with less effort while unlocking new capabilities," citing DSP assistant efficiency gains of up to 97% and satisfaction scores over 90%. He noted a more than doubling of the enterprise customer base in 2025.
  • CFO Sagi Niri reported, "contribution ex-TAC and programmatic revenue to date in Q1 are trending ahead of our initial expectations," and described a positive spending trend by a previously cautious DSP customer. Niri explained, "In Q4, we delivered contribution ex-TAC of $97.8 million, reflecting a 7% year-over-year decrease or a 1% decrease ex political. Programmatic revenue was $94.3 million, down 4% year-over-year, but up 2% ex political." He also noted a 19% year-over-year decline in CTV revenue, but signaled confidence in a rebound: "We feel well positioned for CTV revenue growth in 2026 and beyond."
  • Niri highlighted a share repurchase of 1.44 million shares in Q4 and an approved new buyback program of up to $40 million. He confirmed Nexxen's continued investments in VI, aiming for a 6% equity stake after an additional $15 million investment in Q3 2026.

Outlook

  • For full year 2026, Nexxen expects contribution ex-TAC in the range of $375 million to $390 million, representing over 8% year-over-year growth at the midpoint, and programmatic revenue of $367 million to $381 million, equating to approximately 10% year-over-year growth at the midpoint. Adjusted EBITDA is projected at $122 million to $132 million, with a margin of about 33% at the midpoint.
  • Niri stated, "We expect growth across enterprise self-service, data products and CTV, driven by focused sales execution, our expanded partnership with V and growing adoption of our programmatic smart TV home screen solution."
  • The company signaled ongoing operating leverage, with expectations for OpEx as a percentage of contribution ex-TAC to decrease modestly from 2025, and targeted ongoing investment in data, infrastructure, and AI.

Financial Results

  • Contribution ex-TAC in Q4 was $97.8 million, and programmatic revenue was $94.3 million. Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 was $33.9 million, with a 35% margin as a percentage of contribution ex-TAC. Net cash from operating activities in Q4 was $37.7 million. Cash and cash equivalents stood at $133.3 million as of year-end, with no long-term debt. Non-IFRS diluted EPS was $0.33 in Q4.
  • Data products contribution ex-TAC increased 51% year-over-year. Desktop video revenue increased 21% year-over-year, while mobile video revenue declined 9%. CTV revenue reached $30.1 million in Q4.
  • For the full year 2025, contribution ex-TAC retention rate declined to 92%, while contribution ex-TAC per active customer increased 7% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Matthew Swanson, RBC Capital Markets, asked about AI's impact and CTV growth. Druker explained that AI is shifting user behavior away from browsing, prompting Nexxen to increase focus on CTV and mobile in-app, which are "less affected by AI." He emphasized the new programmatic marketplace for Smart TV on-screen advertising and partnerships with OEMs and DSPs.
  • Laura Martin, Needham & Company, inquired about data revenue and growth drivers for 2026 guidance. Druker noted that data is "integrated in more than 80% of our campaigns" and is a key differentiator. Niri added that growth engines include in-app mobile, VI investment, and the native display home screen ecosystem, stating, "We are quite confident that we can reach this 8% of general growth and almost 10% in our programmatic growth."
  • Andrew Marok, Raymond James, asked about the sustainability of desktop video growth and CTV outlook. Druker identified in-stream as the main revenue source and positioned native Smart TV ads and the partnership with The Trade Desk as future growth levers.
  • Jason Kreyer, Craig-Hallum, sought more details on Q1 channel strength. Druker said growth is "coming from everywhere," citing expanded infrastructure, enhanced enterprise solutions, and a more positive advertising market.
  • Maria Ripps, Canaccord, questioned Olympics-driven demand and enterprise solutions. Druker highlighted resource shifts and talent additions to enterprise sales, emphasizing the long-term growth potential and the role of AI tools in improving buyer experience.
  • Barton Crockett, Rosenblatt, probed the contribution of political advertising and potential acquisitions. Druker said political activity was not yet a major factor in early 2026 but anticipated a "fairly good and rewarding" season. On acquisitions, Druker said, "We are always open to look around and see," but no targets were disclosed.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on sustainability of growth drivers, channel mix, AI impact, and clarity on guidance assumptions. Questions were direct and pressed for specifics, particularly around the rebound in CTV and the durability of growth in desktop video and enterprise.
  • Management's tone was confident, with Druker frequently emphasizing recent momentum, strategic initiatives, and resilience to AI headwinds. Niri was straightforward about Q4 softness but highlighted early Q1 strength: "Contribution ex-TAC and programmatic revenue to date in Q1 are trending ahead of our initial expectations."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from caution and explanation of headwinds to confidence in execution and future prospects. Management responses were more detailed, with fewer defensive or hesitant phrases.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 was raised significantly, with projected contribution ex-TAC growth over 8% and programmatic revenue growth near 10%, compared to a more cautious outlook in Q3 due to a DSP headwind.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward CTV and enterprise, with tangible product launches and expanded partnerships, compared to the prior quarter’s emphasis on building the platform and addressing third-party DSP reliance.
  • Management’s confidence level improved, supported by record Q1-to-date results and broad-based partner activity, in contrast to Q3’s defensive stance following lowered guidance.
  • Analysts' questions in Q4 focused more on forward opportunities and runway, while Q3 featured more probing on risk mitigation and near-term headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Niri noted persistent nonprogrammatic weakness and customer caution due to tariffs and seasonality.
  • Druker acknowledged that CTV and mobile video saw year-over-year revenue declines and that the revenue retention rate dropped due to discontinuing smaller customer relationships.
  • Both executives cited ongoing investments as crucial to maintaining growth and mitigating risks from AI-driven disruption and market shifts.

Final Takeaway

Nexxen’s leadership emphasized that strategic investments and product innovation in AI, CTV, and enterprise solutions are driving a strong start to 2026, with record early months and growth momentum across key channels. Management underscored its confidence in surpassing prior headwinds, supported by expanded partnerships, a robust pipeline, and a more diversified and resilient revenue mix, positioning the company for sustainable growth and industry leadership in programmatic advertising.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Riley Exploration Permian GAAP EPS of $4.02 beats by $1.40, revenue of $97.2M misses by $11.27M
data.description:
• Riley Exploration Permian press release (REPX): Q4 GAAP EPS of $4.02 beats by $1.40.
  • Revenue of $97.2M (-5.3% Y/Y) misses by $11.27M.
  • Generated $65 million of operating cash flow, $85 million of net income, $66 million of Adjusted EBITDAX(1), $1 million of Total Free Cash Flow(1) and $17 million of Upstream Free Cash Flow(1)
  • Incurred total accrual (activity-based) capital expenditures before acquisitions of $50 million ($28 million for upstream)
  • Sold all of our membership interests in Dovetail Midstream LLC, a wholly owned subsidiary of the Company that held certain midstream infrastructure projects in Eddy County, New Mexico for total cash consideration of $123 million, with the right to earn up to an additional $60 million in cash payments contingent upon achieving certain volumetric performance thresholds over a five-year period ("Midstream Sale")
  • Reduced debt outstanding by $120 million with a year-end debt-to-Adjusted EBITDAX(1) ratio of 1.0x(2)
  • Announced the authorization of a stock repurchase program of up to $100 million of the currently outstanding shares of the Company's common stock
  •

data.title: Franklin Resources AUM rises 1.8% in February
data.description:
Franklin Resources (BEN), known as Franklin Templeton, reported  assets under management of $1.74T at Feb. 28, 2026, up 1.8% from $1.71T at the end of January, the company said Wednesday.

The month's AUM reflected the impact of positive markets and long-term net inflows of ~$10B, including ~$1B of long-term net outflows at Western Asset Management. Excluding Western Asset Management, preliminary net inflows were ~$11B.

Equity AUM rose 1.8% to $721.8B during the month, while fixed income AUM of $443.9B increased 0.7% M/M. Alternative AUM rose 1.1% to $278.4B, and multi-asset AUM grew 3.0% to $210.7B.
